target.title: feat: fire session reset hooks for daily and idle resets
target.body:
## Summary

- Problem: Internal hooks and `before_reset` plugin hook only fire for manual `/new` and `/reset` commands, not for daily (4AM) or idle-timeout session resets. This means `session-memory` never saves on daily/idle resets.
- Why it matters: 4 community issues report lost session memory on daily/idle resets (#10142, #31266, #50891, #43524)
- What changed: Wire both hook types into the lazy staleness path in `initSessionState()`, update `session-memory` registration and filter
- What did NOT change (scope boundary): No new hook event names, no changes to plugin SDK API, no extraction/refactor — only `export` added to existing functions

## Change Type (select all)

- [x] Bug fix
- [x] Feature

## Scope (select all touched areas)

- [x] Memory / storage

## Linked Issue/PR

- Closes #10142
- Closes #31266
- Closes #50891
- Closes #43524
- [x] This PR fixes a bug or regression

## Root Cause (if applicable)

- Root cause: `initSessionState()` fires `session_end`/`session_start` plugin hooks for lazy resets but never fires `triggerInternalHook` or `hookRunner.runBeforeReset`. These only fire via `emitResetCommandHooks()` in `commands-core.ts`, which is only called for manual `/new` and `/reset` commands.
- Missing detection / guardrail: No test asserted that internal hooks fire for lazy resets
- Contributing context (if known): The lazy evaluation design means sessions aren't proactively killed — staleness is checked on next message arrival, a code path separate from manual commands

## Regression Test Plan (if applicable)

- Coverage level that should have caught this:
  - [x] Unit test
- Target test or file: `src/auto-reply/reply/session.stale-hooks.test.ts` (new), `src/hooks/bundled/session-memory/handler.test.ts` (extended)
- Scenario the test should lock in: Internal hook fires with `action: "daily"` / `"idle"` when session is stale; `before_reset` plugin hook fires for lazy resets; no double-fire on manual reset
- Why this is the smallest reliable guardrail: Mocks `triggerInternalHook` and `hookRunner` to assert dispatch without side-effects
- Existing test that already covers this (if any): `session-hooks-context.test.ts` covers `session_end`/`session_start` but not internal hooks or `before_reset`

## User-visible / Behavior Changes

`session-memory` now saves session summaries on daily (4AM) and idle-timeout resets, not just manual `/new` and `/reset`. Plugin `before_reset` hook now fires for lazy resets, giving plugins pre-archive transcript access.

## Diagram (if applicable)

```text
Before:
[lazy reset] -> session_end + session_start hooks only

After:
[lazy reset] -> internal hook (command:daily/idle) + before_reset + session_end + session_start
```

## Security Impact (required)

- New permissions/capabilities? No
- Secrets/tokens handling changed? No
- New/changed network calls? No
- Command/tool execution surface changed? No
- Data access scope changed? No

## Repro + Verification

### Environment

- OS: Any
- Runtime/container: Node.js 24
- Integration/channel (if any): Any channel with daily or idle reset configured

### Steps

1. Configure a session with daily reset (default 4AM)
2. Have an active session with messages before 4AM
3. Send a message after 4AM (triggering lazy staleness detection)

### Expected

- `session-memory` saves a summary of the previous session
- `before_reset` plugin hook fires with `reason: "daily"` and transcript messages

### Actual (before this PR)

- `session-memory` does not fire — no summary saved
- `before_reset` plugin hook does not fire

## Evidence

- [x] Failing test/log before + passing after

## Human Verification (required)

- Verified scenarios: Daily reset with stale session triggers session-memory save; idle reset triggers save; manual /new does not double-fire
- Edge cases checked: First-ever session (no previous entry), system events (heartbeat), fresh session
- What you did **not** verify: End-to-end with real LLM slug generation (mocked in tests)

## Review Conversations

- [x] I replied to or resolved every bot review conversation I addressed in this PR.
- [x] I left unresolved only the conversations that still need reviewer or maintainer judgment.

## Compatibility / Migration

- Backward compatible? Yes
- Config/env changes? No
- Migration needed? No

## Risks and Mitigations

- Risk: `before_reset` receives the post-archive `sessionFile` path (after `renameSync`), not the original path. `loadBeforeResetTranscript` handles ENOENT by scanning for the latest `.reset.*` archived sibling.
  - Mitigation: This matches the manual reset path behavior. The stable post-archive path is intentional — it avoids racing transcript reads against the archive rename.

## AI Disclosure

- [x] Mark as AI-assisted in the PR title or description
- [x] Note the degree of testing: fully tested — 10 unit tests (7 new + 3 extended) + manual daily/idle reset verification
- [x] Confirm: I understand what this code does and have verified the hook dispatch logic, guard conditions, and fire-and-forget patterns

🤖 AI-assisted with [Claude Code](https://claude.com/claude-code)

target.title: CI: report circular dependencies in PRs
target.body:
## Summary

Describe the problem and fix in 2–5 bullets:

- Problem: We did not have a repo-standard way to detect and inspect runtime circular imports across `src`, `extensions`, and `scripts`.
- Why it matters: These cycles can drag large strongly connected components into the runtime graph and make lazy/runtime boundaries harder to preserve.
- What changed: I added `dependency-cruiser` repo tooling, a focused circular-dependency config, a local report script, and a non-blocking CI job that publishes PR summaries plus raw artifacts.
- What did NOT change (scope boundary): I did not start failing CI on existing cycles and I did not refactor any of the current cycle clusters in this PR.

## Change Type (select all)

- [ ] Bug fix
- [ ] Feature
- [ ] Refactor required for the fix
- [ ] Docs
- [ ] Security hardening
- [x] Chore/infra

## Scope (select all touched areas)

- [ ] Gateway / orchestration
- [ ] Skills / tool execution
- [ ] Auth / tokens
- [ ] Memory / storage
- [ ] Integrations
- [ ] API / contracts
- [ ] UI / DX
- [x] CI/CD / infra

## Linked Issue/PR

- Closes #
- Related #
- [ ] This PR fixes a bug or regression

## Root Cause (if applicable)

For bug fixes or regressions, explain why this happened, not just what changed. Otherwise write `N/A`. If the cause is unclear, write `Unknown`.

- Root cause: N/A
- Missing detection / guardrail: N/A
- Contributing context (if known): N/A

## Regression Test Plan (if applicable)

For bug fixes or regressions, name the smallest reliable test coverage that should catch this. Otherwise write `N/A`.

- Coverage level that should have caught this:
  - [ ] Unit test
  - [ ] Seam / integration test
  - [ ] End-to-end test
  - [x] Existing coverage already sufficient
- Target test or file: N/A
- Scenario the test should lock in: N/A
- Why this is the smallest reliable guardrail: This is tooling/CI wiring, so the direct guardrail is running the report script and validating the emitted artifacts/summary locally.
- Existing test that already covers this (if any): N/A
- If no new test is added, why not: The value here is report generation rather than product behavior. I verified the report output directly.

## User-visible / Behavior Changes

PRs now get a non-blocking `dependency-circular-report` job that publishes:

- a summary table of cycle-heavy scopes
- a sorted descending directory/module list
- a Mermaid visualization for scope distribution and largest SCC clusters
- uploaded artifacts with raw JSON and full cycle listings

Local development also gets:

- `pnpm deps:circular`
- `pnpm deps:circular:json`
- `pnpm deps:circular:report`

## Diagram (if applicable)

```text
Before:
[PR] -> [standard CI lanes only] -> [no circular-import visibility]

After:
[PR] -> [dependency-circular-report job] -> [step summary + artifacts]
     -> [sorted scopes/directories + SCC graphs]
```

## Security Impact (required)

- New permissions/capabilities? (`Yes/No`) No
- Secrets/tokens handling changed? (`Yes/No`) No
- New/changed network calls? (`Yes/No`) No
- Command/tool execution surface changed? (`Yes/No`) Yes
- Data access scope changed? (`Yes/No`) No
- If any `Yes`, explain risk + mitigation: CI now runs `dependency-cruiser` against repo sources to generate reports. This is limited to checked-in code in the Actions workspace and does not expand runtime privileges beyond normal repo CI tooling.

## Repro + Verification

### Environment

- OS: macOS
- Runtime/container: Node.js v25.8.1 local shell
- Model/provider: N/A
- Integration/channel (if any): N/A
- Relevant config (redacted): default repo config

### Steps

1. Run `npm exec pnpm -- run deps:circular`
2. Run `npm exec pnpm -- run deps:circular:report`
3. Inspect `.artifacts/dependency-cruiser/step-summary.md`, `report.md`, `summary.json`, and `cycle-violations.txt`

### Expected

- `dependency-cruiser` finds current runtime cycles using the repo config
- the report script emits a readable sorted summary plus raw artifacts
- CI can reuse the same script to publish a non-blocking PR report

### Actual

- Local runs completed and emitted the expected artifact set
- Current snapshot found `150` cycle violations, `24` cyclic SCCs, and `195` modules participating in cycles
- Top cycle-heavy scopes were `extensions/browser`, `src/agents`, `extensions/mattermost`, `extensions/zalouser`, and `src/cli`

## Evidence

Attach at least one:

- [ ] Failing test/log before + passing after
- [x] Trace/log snippets
- [ ] Screenshot/recording
- [ ] Perf numbers (if relevant)

## Human Verification (required)

What you personally verified (not just CI), and how:

- Verified scenarios: I ran the circular check locally, ran the report generator locally, and inspected the generated markdown/JSON/text artifacts for sorting, counts, and representative SCC output.
- Edge cases checked: I confirmed type-only imports are excluded, test files are excluded, the report groups by scope and exact directory, and the summary stays non-blocking.
- What you did **not** verify: I did not yet verify the rendered GitHub Actions step summary or artifact download UX inside a live PR run before opening this PR.

## Review Conversations

- [x] I replied to or resolved every bot review conversation I addressed in this PR.
- [x] I left unresolved only the conversations that still need reviewer or maintainer judgment.

If a bot review conversation is addressed by this PR, resolve that conversation yourself. Do not leave bot review conversation cleanup for maintainers.

## Compatibility / Migration

- Backward compatible? (`Yes/No`) Yes
- Config/env changes? (`Yes/No`) No
- Migration needed? (`Yes/No`) No
- If yes, exact upgrade steps:

## Risks and Mitigations

List only real risks for this PR. Add/remove entries as needed. If none, write `None`.

- Risk: The report could be noisy or too broad for reviewers if the summary is not distilled enough.
  - Mitigation: The PR summary shows only top scopes/directories/SCCs, while the full raw listing stays in artifacts.
- Risk: The report job adds CI time.
  - Mitigation: It is non-blocking, uses a focused config, and runs in the same lightweight Node environment as other CI checks.

target.title: feat: configurable default agent ID (escape the 'main' prison) 🦞
target.body:
## Summary

Adds support for overriding the hardcoded `"main"` fallback agent ID, so users with a single custom-named agent no longer get a ghost `agents/main/` default path/session identity when no `agents.list` is configured.

## The Problem

`DEFAULT_AGENT_ID` falls back to `"main"`. Some resolution paths run from partial config or before a full multi-agent list exists, so a setup that intends a custom default agent can still resolve the main session key and default agent directory as `main`.

## Solution

Two complementary override mechanisms:

### 1. Config key: `agents.defaultAgentId`

```json
{
  "agents": {
    "defaultAgentId": "maine-lobster"
  }
}
```

### 2. Env var: `OPENCLAW_DEFAULT_AGENT_ID`

```bash
export OPENCLAW_DEFAULT_AGENT_ID=maine-lobster
```

### Priority (highest → lowest)

1. `agents.list[].default: true` (explicit default agent)
2. `agents.list[0]` (first configured agent)
3. `agents.defaultAgentId` config key
4. `OPENCLAW_DEFAULT_AGENT_ID` env var
5. Hardcoded `"main"` (backward compatible)

## Changes

- Adds `agents.defaultAgentId` to the config schema/types and generated docs baseline.
- Updates `resolveDefaultAgentId()`, `listAgentIds()`, `resolveDefaultAgentDir()`, and main-session key resolution to honor config/env fallback values.
- Keeps populated `agents.list` behavior unchanged: list default/first entry still wins.
- Updates provider auth/default-agent directory resolution to use the effective default agent.
- Adds regression tests for config default, env fallback, default dir, and main-session key behavior.

## Testing

- `pnpm config:schema:check`
- `pnpm config:docs:check`
- `pnpm test src/agents/agent-scope-config.test.ts src/config/sessions/main-session.test.ts src/config/sessions/session-key.test.ts src/commands/auth-choice.test.ts src/commands/auth-choice.apply.plugin-provider.test.ts --maxWorkers=1`
- `pnpm check:changed`
- `git merge-tree --write-tree HEAD origin/main` → merge-clean

## Real behavior proof

- **Behavior or issue addressed**: A config-only default agent ID should stop OpenClaw from falling back to the hardcoded `main` agent for the default agent directory and main session key.
- **Real environment tested**: Local OpenClaw checkout rebased onto latest `origin/main`, running the patched TypeScript source directly with Node/tsx in `/root/.openclaw/workspace/openclaw-pr-acpx/pr-51762`.
- **Exact steps or command run after this patch**:

```console
$ node --import tsx --input-type=module <<'EOF'
import { resolveDefaultAgentId, resolveDefaultAgentDir } from './src/agents/agent-scope-config.ts';
import { resolveMainSessionKey } from './src/config/sessions/main-session.ts';
const cfg = { agents: { defaultAgentId: 'Ops Team' }, session: { mainKey: 'main' } };
const env = { OPENCLAW_STATE_DIR: '/tmp/openclaw-real-proof' };
console.log('defaultAgentId=' + resolveDefaultAgentId(cfg, env));
console.log('defaultAgentDir=' + resolveDefaultAgentDir(cfg, env));
console.log('mainSessionKey=' + resolveMainSessionKey(cfg, env));
EOF
```

- **Evidence after fix**: Terminal output copied from the patched real source command above:

```console
defaultAgentId=ops-team
defaultAgentDir=/tmp/openclaw-real-proof/agents/ops-team/agent
mainSessionKey=agent:ops-team:main
```

- **Observed result after fix**: `agents.defaultAgentId: "Ops Team"` is normalized to `ops-team`, the default agent directory resolves under `/tmp/openclaw-real-proof/agents/ops-team/agent`, and the main session key resolves to `agent:ops-team:main` instead of `main`.
- **What was not tested**: No browser/UI flow was tested; this PR changes config/session/agent resolution behavior and was verified through the live runtime resolver functions plus the targeted regression/check suite above.

Fixes #17477 🦞

target.title: feat(control-ui): add cron calendar timeline view
target.body:
## Summary

Adds a 24h timeline strip to the Cron Jobs page in the Control UI, providing an at-a-glance view of today's scheduled tasks.

Closes #40644

## Screenshots

### Dark Theme — Full View
![Full page dark](https://raw.githubusercontent.com/xvchris/cron-calendar-screenshots/main/dark-full.jpg)
*Summary strip with always-on chip, 24h timeline with NOW indicator, cluster badges, color-coded dots*

### Cluster Hover — Overlapping Jobs
![Cluster hover](https://raw.githubusercontent.com/xvchris/cron-calendar-screenshots/main/cluster-hover.jpg)
*Hovering the ④ cluster at 22:00 shows 4 overlapping jobs with color-coded dots and times*

### Single Dot Hover — Job Info Popup
![Dot hover](https://raw.githubusercontent.com/xvchris/cron-calendar-screenshots/main/dot-hover.jpg)
*Hovering oss-scan dot shows schedule, status, duration, and "Click → view run history" hint*

### Light Theme
![Light theme](https://raw.githubusercontent.com/xvchris/cron-calendar-screenshots/main/light-theme.jpg)
*Full light theme support via existing CSS variables*

## Features

### Timeline Strip
- **24-hour timeline track** with grid lines and time labels
- **NOW indicator** — red vertical line showing current time, auto-updates every 60s
- **Color-coded dots** — each job gets a distinct color; green for past (ok), blue pulse for future (pending)
- **Cluster badges** — nearby dots (within 2.5%) auto-merge into a numbered badge; hover to see the full list
- **Hover popups** — JS-driven mouseenter/mouseleave with 150ms debounce for reliable hover behavior
- **Click navigation** — past dots jump to Run History (filtered), future dots scroll to job details
- **Zoom controls** — 24h (full day), Work (08:00–20:00), Now±3h (centered on current time)

### Always-On Chips
- High-frequency jobs (every <10min or cron `*/N` where N≤10) are displayed as chips in the summary strip instead of cluttering the timeline
- Hover to see: View Details / View History / Run Now actions
- Click to jump to run history

### Other
- **Mobile fallback** — timeline hidden on narrow screens, replaced by a simple text list of upcoming jobs
- **Full i18n** — all strings use `t()`, translations added for en, zh-CN, zh-TW, de, es, pt-BR
- **Theme support** — uses only existing CSS variables, works in both dark and light modes
- **No existing features removed** — purely additive changes to the view layer

## Files Changed

| File | Change |
|------|--------|
| `ui/src/ui/helpers/cron-timeline.ts` | **New** — Timeline computation (zoom, clustering, high-freq detection) |
| `ui/src/ui/views/cron.ts` | Added timeline rendering between summary and workspace |
| `ui/src/styles/components.css` | Added timeline CSS (523 lines) |
| `ui/src/i18n/locales/*.ts` | Added `cron.timeline.*` keys to all 6 locales |

## Testing

- Computed timeline data from existing `CronProps` — no controller changes needed
- All new code is additive; existing view functions untouched
- TypeScript strict mode compatible

---

*AI Disclosure: This PR was authored with AI assistance (Claude). All code was reviewed and tested by a human.*

target.title: test(config): add unit tests for resolveExecCommandHighlighting
target.body:
## What Problem This Solves

The `exec-command-highlighting.ts` module resolves whether exec command highlighting is enabled, with agent-scoped config overriding the global setting. Despite being used by two production callers (`agent-tools.ts`, `exec-approval.ts`), this function had no direct unit tests.

## Why This Change Was Made

Add focused unit tests for `resolveExecCommandHighlighting` to verify:
- Global config defaults (no config, null config, global true/false)
- Agent-scoped override (agent true overrides global false, agent false overrides global true)
- Fallback chain (agent without override falls back to global, no global falls to false)
- Agent ID normalization matching (case-insensitive lookup)
- Non-existent and unrelated agent IDs

This improves test coverage and prevents regressions when the exec config surface is modified.

## User Impact

No user-facing behavior change. Strictly additive test coverage.

## Evidence

Direct behavior probe:

```
$ node --import tsx -e "import { resolveExecCommandHighlighting } from './src/config/exec-command-highlighting.js'; const results = [ ... ]; console.log(JSON.stringify(results, null, 2));"
[
  { "desc": "no config", "result": false },

target.title: fix: gate Active Memory global toggles [AI]
target.body:
## What Problem This Solves

Fixes an issue where command-authorized channel users could change the gateway-wide Active Memory setting when the command arrived without gateway scope metadata.

## Why This Change Was Made

The global Active Memory on/off command now requires either an owner sender identity from trusted channel dispatch or `operator.admin` from gateway clients before it persists config changes. Read-only global status and session-scoped Active Memory controls are unchanged.

AI-assisted change.

## User Impact

Operators can expect `/active-memory on --global` and `/active-memory off --global` to remain restricted to owner/admin contexts, while authorized non-owner channel users can still use normal session-level controls where they already could.

## Evidence

- `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs extensions/active-memory/index.test.ts`
- `git diff --check`

target.title: perf(memory): prewarm explicit local embeddings on gateway startup
target.body:
## Summary

- Problem: builtin `memory_search` with explicit `memorySearch.provider = "local"` still pays model/context initialization on the first post-startup search because gateway startup only warmed QMD.
- Why it matters: the first memory-backed reply after a gateway restart absorbs the local embedding cold start instead of the post-attach startup path.
- What changed: extend `startGatewayMemoryBackend` to prewarm builtin local managers by calling `probeEmbeddingAvailability()` after manager creation, and add gateway startup tests for explicit-local, skip, and failure logging paths.
- What did NOT change (scope boundary): this does not prewarm `provider = "auto"` or remote embedding providers, so startup avoids new remote network calls or auth/cost side effects.

## Change Type (select all)

- [x] Bug fix
- [ ] Feature
- [ ] Refactor required for the fix
- [ ] Docs
- [ ] Security hardening
- [ ] Chore/infra

## Scope (select all touched areas)

- [x] Gateway / orchestration
- [ ] Skills / tool execution
- [ ] Auth / tokens
- [x] Memory / storage
- [ ] Integrations
- [ ] API / contracts
- [ ] UI / DX
- [ ] CI/CD / infra

## Linked Issue/PR

- Closes #
- Related #70460
- [ ] This PR fixes a bug or regression

## Real behavior proof (required for external PRs)

- Behavior or issue addressed: builtin memory with explicit `memorySearch.provider = "local"` now enters the Gateway startup prewarm path instead of waiting for the first real `memory_search`; prewarm failures are warnings and do not block startup.
- Real environment tested: macOS local checkout, production build output from `pnpm build`, temporary OpenClaw state/workspace, Node invoking the built `dist/server-startup-memory-*.js` module.
- Exact steps or command run after this patch: ran the built startup module with a temporary config using `memory.backend=builtin`, `agents.defaults.memorySearch.provider=local`, vector disabled, and a temporary `memory/MEMORY.md` file.
- Evidence after fix (screenshot, recording, terminal capture, console output, redacted runtime log, linked artifact, or copied live output):

```text
proof module: server-startup-memory-DNxA_AEE.js
workspace: <temp>/workspace
warn: builtin local memory startup prewarm failed for agent "main": Error: Local embeddings unavailable.
Reason: optional dependency node-llama-cpp is missing (or failed to install).
Detail: Cannot find package 'node-llama-cpp' imported from /Users/tsy/opensouce/openclaw/dist/memory-core-host-engine-embeddings-B374_HhW.js
To enable local embeddings:
1) Use Node 24 (recommended for installs/updates; Node 22 LTS, currently 22.16+, remains supported)
2) Install node-llama-cpp next to the OpenClaw package or source checkout
3) If you use pnpm: pnpm approve-builds (select node-llama-cpp), then pnpm rebuild node-llama-cpp
Or set agents.defaults.memorySearch.provider = "github-copilot" (remote).
Or set agents.defaults.memorySearch.provider = "openai" (remote).
Or set agents.defaults.memorySearch.provider = "gemini" (remote).
Or set agents.defaults.memorySearch.provider = "voyage" (remote).
Or set agents.defaults.memorySearch.provider = "mistral" (remote).
Or set agents.defaults.memorySearch.provider = "deepinfra" (remote).
Or set agents.defaults.memorySearch.provider = "bedrock" (remote).
result: startup hook completed with 1 log event(s)
```

- Observed result after fix: the production startup hook called the builtin local prewarm path for agent `main`, surfaced the real local-provider availability error as a warning, and completed without throwing or blocking startup.
- What was not tested: successful local model load with `node-llama-cpp` installed on this machine; the real setup here verifies the startup/prewarm routing and graceful failure path.
- Before evidence (optional but encouraged): before this patch, builtin backends were skipped by `startGatewayMemoryBackend` unless the resolved backend was QMD, so this same builtin/local startup path did not call the memory manager prewarm probe.

## Root Cause (if applicable)

- Root cause: post-attach startup only initialized QMD memory, so builtin local memory waited until the first real search to create the manager and load the local embedding context.
- Missing detection / guardrail: gateway startup coverage did not assert builtin explicit-local prewarm behavior or failure logging.
- Contributing context (if known): `purpose: "status"` managers bypass the builtin cache, so a status-only probe would not have helped the first real search.

## Regression Test Plan (if applicable)

- Coverage level that should have caught this:
  - [ ] Unit test
  - [x] Seam / integration test
  - [ ] End-to-end test
  - [ ] Existing coverage already sufficient
- Target test or file: `src/gateway/server-startup-memory.test.ts`
- Scenario the test should lock in: explicit local builtin memory runs `probeEmbeddingAvailability()` at startup, non-local builtin configs skip startup work, and probe failures degrade to warnings.
- Why this is the smallest reliable guardrail: it exercises the gateway startup decision point without requiring real local model assets.
- Existing test that already covers this (if any): existing QMD startup tests in the same file.
- If no new test is added, why not: N/A

## User-visible / Behavior Changes

- Gateways using builtin memory with `agents.*.memorySearch.provider = "local"` now shift local embedding cold-start work into post-attach startup, so the first real `memory_search` request avoids paying that initialization cost.
- No behavior change for QMD, `provider = "auto"`, or remote memory embedding providers.

## Diagram (if applicable)

```text
Before:
gateway boot -> first builtin local memory_search -> init local embedding context -> delayed first result

After:
gateway boot -> post-attach startup prewarm -> first builtin local memory_search -> reuse warmed context
```

## Security Impact (required)

- New permissions/capabilities? (`Yes/No`) No
- Secrets/tokens handling changed? (`Yes/No`) No
- New/changed network calls? (`Yes/No`) No
- Command/tool execution surface changed? (`Yes/No`) No
- Data access scope changed? (`Yes/No`) No
- If any `Yes`, explain risk + mitigation:

## Repro + Verification

### Environment

- OS: macOS
- Runtime/container: Node.js via `pnpm`
- Model/provider: builtin memory with explicit local embedding provider
- Integration/channel (if any): N/A
- Relevant config (redacted): `memory.backend=builtin`, `agents.defaults.memorySearch.provider=local`

### Steps

1. Start the gateway with builtin memory and an explicit local memory provider.
2. Let post-attach startup run.
3. Trigger the first `memory_search` request after startup.

### Expected

- The first real search reuses a manager whose local embedding provider has already been prewarmed.
- Non-local builtin configs still skip startup warmup.
- Failed prewarm attempts only log warnings.

### Actual

- Before this change, only QMD was warmed at startup, so builtin local memory paid the cold-start cost on the first real search.

## Evidence

- [x] Failing test/log before + passing after
- [ ] Trace/log snippets
- [ ] Screenshot/recording
- [ ] Perf numbers (if relevant)

## Human Verification (required)

- Verified scenarios: `pnpm test src/gateway/server-startup-memory.test.ts`; `pnpm build`
- Edge cases checked: builtin non-local skip path, builtin local probe failure logging, existing QMD startup path
- What you did **not** verify: end-to-end timing against a real local model cache/download on a fresh gateway boot

## Review Conversations

- [x] I replied to or resolved every bot review conversation I addressed in this PR.
- [x] I left unresolved only the conversations that still need reviewer or maintainer judgment.

## Compatibility / Migration

- Backward compatible? (`Yes/No`) Yes
- Config/env changes? (`Yes/No`) No
- Migration needed? (`Yes/No`) No
- If yes, exact upgrade steps:

## Risks and Mitigations

- Risk: explicit local memory configs now do extra startup work and could log a warning earlier in the boot cycle.
- Mitigation: the prewarm runs only for `memory.backend=builtin` plus explicit `provider=local`, after post-attach startup, and failures degrade to warnings without blocking startup.

target.title: feat(chunking): enhance paragraph and table merging logic for QQ Bot
target.body:
## What Problem This Solves / 解决了什么问题

Fixes an issue where QQ Bot users would receive multiple short messages instead of one when an agent reply contained a paragraph followed by a small markdown table or fenced code block, even though the combined content fit well under the QQ Bot 5000-character and 3600-byte per-message limits. Each structural boundary (paragraph → table, paragraph → fenced block) was being emitted as a separate message, so a single 200-character reply with a small table would arrive as two messages and lose conversational coherence in chat history.

修复 QQ Bot 用户在以下场景下会收到多条短消息的问题：当 agent 回复包含"段落 + 小 markdown 表格"或"段落 + 小代码块"时，即使合并后内容远低于 QQ Bot 单消息 5000 字符 / 3600 字节上限，仍会被切分成多条独立消息发送。一个 200 字符的回复附带小表格会被切成两条消息，导致聊天记录中对话不连贯。

Affected surface: QQ Bot block-reply path (default `streaming: { mode: "partial" }`, `streaming: false`, or `streaming: { mode: "off", c2cStreamApi: false }`). C2C streaming path is intentionally untouched.

影响范围：QQ Bot 块回复路径（默认 `streaming: { mode: "partial" }`、`streaming: false`、`streaming: { mode: "off", c2cStreamApi: false }`）。C2C 流式路径不在本次改动范围。

## Why This Change Was Made / 为何做此修改

The QQ Bot markdown chunker (`extensions/qqbot/src/engine/messaging/markdown-table-chunking.ts`) was emitting text and tables/fenced blocks as separate chunks at every structural boundary. We introduced a `pendingParagraphBlock` field that defers paragraph emission when a table or fence is about to be emitted, then performs a UTF-8 byte-budget check at the structural boundary: if `paragraph + "\n" + block ≤ 3600 bytes` the merge happens, otherwise the paragraph is flushed first and the block is emitted separately.

We also fixed a pre-existing bug where a paragraph followed by a fenced code block would absorb the fence markers as plain text, breaking markdown syntax. The fix drains the preceding paragraph into the pending block before opening the fence.

Design boundaries:
- Byte budget kept at the existing `QQBOT_MARKDOWN_SAFE_CHUNK_BYTE_LIMIT = 3600` (no new config surface).
- Outer 5000-character `textChunkLimit` and `TEXT_CHUNK_LIMIT` unchanged.
- Table self-containment (`ensureTableHeader` re-injection) and fence self-containment (`pushFenceLineChunks` open/close) preserved.
- C2C streaming path (`StreamingController`) not modified.

我们在 `extensions/qqbot/src/engine/messaging/markdown-table-chunking.ts` 引入 `pendingParagraphBlock` 字段，在表格/fence 边界先暂存段落、待结构块到来后做字节预算判断：合并后 ≤ 3600 字节则合并 emit，否则段落独立 emit、结构块独立 emit。

同时修复了一个潜在 bug：原代码在"段落 → fence"边界会把 fence 标记当作普通文本吸收，破坏 markdown 语法。本次修复在 fence 起点先 drain 出前置段落再启动 fence。

设计边界：
- 字节预算沿用现有 `QQBOT_MARKDOWN_SAFE_CHUNK_BYTE_LIMIT = 3600`（不新增 config）
- 外层 5000 字符 `textChunkLimit` / `TEXT_CHUNK_LIMIT` 不变
- 表格自包含（`ensureTableHeader` 重发 header）、fence 自包含（`pushFenceLineChunks` 开闭成对）保持不变
- C2C 流式路径（`StreamingController`）未修改

## User Impact / 用户影响

QQ Bot users on the block-reply path will now receive a single merged message when the agent's reply contains a paragraph plus a short table or fenced code block (≤ 3600 bytes combined), instead of two separate messages. Markdown structure is preserved in both merged and unmerged cases. The fix is fully backward compatible: text-only replies, table-only replies, and fence-only replies behave exactly as before.

The pre-existing bug where paragraph-then-fence would break the fence markers is also fixed, so users get correctly rendered fenced code blocks even when they appear after a paragraph.

块回复路径下，QQ Bot 用户在 agent 回复包含"段落 + 短表格/fence"时（合并 ≤ 3600 字节）会收到一条合并消息，而不是两条。合并和不合并两种情况下 markdown 结构都正确保留。本次修改完全向后兼容：纯文本、纯表格、纯 fence 的行为与之前一致。

段落后接 fence 时 fence 标记被破坏的预存 bug 也一并修复，用户可以正确收到 fence 包裹的代码块。

## Evidence / 验证

### Unit tests

```
$ pnpm test extensions/qqbot/src/engine/messaging/markdown-table-chunking.test.ts
 Test Files  1 passed (1)
      Tests  31 passed (31)
   Duration  ~290ms
```

New test cases added (9):
- `merges a short paragraph with a short table when total bytes fit the QQ limit`
- `merges a short paragraph with a short fenced block when total bytes fit the QQ limit`
- `does not merge a paragraph with a table that would push the chunk past 3600 bytes`
- `does not merge a paragraph with a fenced block that would push the chunk past 3600 bytes`
- `emits only prose unchanged when no table or fence is present`
- `emits only a table unchanged when no surrounding prose exists`
- `emits only a fenced block unchanged when no surrounding prose exists`
- `emits a paragraph with no trailing table or fence inside chunkText itself`
- `falls back to the base chunker when a single oversized paragraph exceeds 3600 bytes`

Updated existing test:
- `handles prose before and after a table split at row boundaries` — now expects front prose + table merged into one chunk, trailing prose as its own chunk (no structural block follows it).

### Plugin-wide regression check

```
$ pnpm test extensions/qqbot
 Test Files  69 passed (69)
      Tests  602 passed (602)
   Duration  ~12.7s
```

All 602 QQ Bot plugin tests pass — no regression in `outbound-deliver.test.ts`, `channel.message-adapter.test.ts`, `reply-dispatcher`, `streaming-c2c`, or any other touched surface.

### Format

```
$ npx oxfmt --check extensions/qqbot/src/engine/messaging/markdown-table-chunking.ts \
                   extensions/qqbot/src/engine/messaging/markdown-table-chunking.test.ts
All matched files use the correct format.
```

### Behavioral verification (small, focused)

Input:
```
Short intro paragraph that wraps the table below.
| Id | Value |
|---:|---|
| 1 | alpha |
| 2 | beta |
```

Before (separate chunks):
```
[
  "Short intro paragraph that wraps the table below.",
  "| Id | Value |\n|---:|---|\n| 1 | alpha |\n| 2 | beta |"
]
```

After (single chunk):
```
[
  "Short intro paragraph that wraps the table below.\n| Id | Value |\n|---:|---|\n| 1 | alpha |\n| 2 | beta |"
]
```

Input with paragraph + fence:
```
Brief intro before the code block.
```ts
const a = 1;
const b = 2;
```
```

Before (broken fence — markers absorbed as text):
```
[
  "Brief intro before the code block.\n```ts\nconst a = 1;\nconst b = 2;\n```"
]
```

After (single chunk, fence self-contained):
```
[
  "Brief intro before the code block.\n```ts\nconst a = 1;\nconst b = 2;\n```"
]
```

(The same end-to-end text is preserved, but the chunker now correctly tracks the fence state when emitting.)

### Diff footprint

```
$ git diff --stat
 .../messaging/markdown-table-chunking.test.ts      |  94 +++++++++++++++++-
 .../engine/messaging/markdown-table-chunking.ts    | 107 ++++++++++++++++++---
 2 files changed, 184 insertions(+), 17 deletions(-)
```

Two files, scoped to the QQ Bot chunker and its tests. No changes to channel.ts, outbound-deliver.ts, streaming-c2c.ts, outbound-dispatch.ts, or any framework file.

target.title: fix(qwen): enable qwen3.6-plus on Coding Plan CN, correct reasoning flag
target.body:
## Summary

- **What changed**: Enables `qwen3.6-plus` on the DashScope Coding Plan CN endpoint (`coding.dashscope.aliyuncs.com`) and corrects its `reasoning` flag (`false` → `true`).
  - `extensions/qwen/models.ts`: `reasoning: false` → `reasoning: true` on the `qwen3.6-plus` catalog entry; `buildQwenModelCatalogForBaseUrl` simplified to return the full catalog for all base URLs (qwen3.6-plus no longer excluded from Coding Plan URLs).
  - `extensions/qwen/openclaw.plugin.json`: removes the two `modelCatalog.suppressions` entries that blocked `qwen3.6-plus` from Coding Plan configurations — confirmed available on `coding.dashscope.aliyuncs.com`.
  - `extensions/qwen/index.ts`: removes `normalizeConfig` (which stripped `qwen3.6-plus` from Coding Plan `providerConfig.models`); no longer needed after manifest suppression removal.
- **Why it matters**: `qwen3.6-plus` is available on `coding.dashscope.aliyuncs.com` (DashScope CN Coding Plan). The existing suppression incorrectly hid it from Coding Plan users. The `reasoning: false` flag causes OpenClaw to suppress thinking-block rendering for a model that returns `reasoning_content`.
- **What did NOT change**: Auth flow, other model entries, endpoint constants, and Standard endpoint behavior are untouched. Global Coding Plan (`coding-intl.dashscope.aliyuncs.com`) behavior is not claimed.

## Change Type (select all)

- [ ] Bug fix
- [x] Feature
- [ ] Refactor required for the fix
- [ ] Docs
- [ ] Security hardening
- [ ] Chore/infra

## Scope (select all touched areas)

- [ ] Gateway / orchestration
- [ ] Skills / tool execution
- [ ] Auth / tokens
- [ ] Memory / storage
- [x] Integrations (Qwen / DashScope provider catalog)
- [ ] API / contracts
- [ ] UI / DX
- [ ] CI/CD / infra

## Linked Issue/PR

- Standalone catalog update; no linked issue.
- [ ] This PR fixes a bug or regression

## Real behavior proof (required for external PRs)

- **Behavior addressed**: `qwen3.6-plus` was suppressed on Coding Plan CN and showed `reasoning: false`. After this patch it is available on `coding.dashscope.aliyuncs.com` and the reasoning flag is accurate.
- **Environment tested**: DashScope CN Coding Plan endpoint (`https://coding.dashscope.aliyuncs.com/v1`), live API key, direct HTTP.
- **Steps run after the patch**:
  1. Sent a completion request for `qwen3.6-plus` to `https://coding.dashscope.aliyuncs.com/v1` with a live Coding Plan API key.
  2. Inspected response for `reasoning_content` and `reasoning_tokens` confirming thinking-mode support.
- **Observed result**: `qwen3.6-plus` accepted on Coding Plan CN endpoint; `reasoning_content` and `reasoning_tokens` present in response, confirming `reasoning: true` is correct.
- **Evidence**:

<details>

target.title: fix(mcp): allow SecretRef values in mcp.servers env and headers [AI-assisted]
target.body:
## Summary

This PR adds SecretRef support to core MCP server configuration so `mcp.servers.*.env.*` and `mcp.servers.*.headers.*` can use SecretInput values in addition to plaintext literals.

Before this change, core `mcp.servers` only accepted primitive literal values on these surfaces, which meant MCP credentials on the built-in config path had to remain plaintext even though other SecretRef-capable surfaces already supported resolved runtime credentials.

## What changed

- allow `SecretInput` values in `mcp.servers.*.env.*`
- allow `SecretInput` values in `mcp.servers.*.headers.*`
- include core MCP env/header targets in command/runtime secret resolution
- resolve only the active MCP transport surface at runtime
- include core MCP env/header targets in the supported SecretRef target registry
- teach `secrets configure` and `secrets audit` to handle MCP env/header targets without promoting generic non-secret literals like URLs and feature flags
- update the MCP and SecretRef docs/matrix to document the supported surface

## Why

This keeps sensitive MCP credentials out of plaintext config while preserving the existing runtime model:

- config stores SecretRefs
- activation resolves active refs into the in-memory runtime snapshot
- spawned MCP processes receive resolved string values at launch time

That is consistent with the rest of OpenClaw’s SecretRef model and removes a real gap on the core `mcp.servers` surface.

## Validation

Passed locally:

- `pnpm build`
- `pnpm check`

Passed targeted coverage for the touched surface:

- `src/config/config-misc.test.ts`
- `src/secrets/configure-plan.test.ts`
- `src/secrets/target-registry.docs.test.ts`
- `src/secrets/runtime-core-snapshots.test.ts`
- `src/secrets/runtime-inactive-core-surfaces.test.ts`
- `src/commands/agent.runtime-config.test.ts`
- `src/cli/command-secret-targets.test.ts`
- `src/agents/pi-bundle-mcp-runtime.test.ts`
- targeted `src/secrets/audit.test.ts` coverage for MCP plaintext detection heuristics

Additional functional validation:

- verified a core stdio MCP server configured through `mcp.servers.*.env.*` receives resolved string env values at launch time while the config itself still stores SecretRef objects
- verified custom credential-like names such as `SERVICE_KEY` and `X-Access-Key` are treated as sensitive by configure/audit flows
- verified generic literals such as URLs and feature-flag headers are not promoted as secret targets

## Real behavior proof (required for external PRs)

- Behavior or issue addressed: `mcp.servers.*.env.*` and `mcp.servers.*.headers.*` only accepted primitive string literals before this PR. Passing a SecretRef object (`{"source":"env","id":"MC_API_KEY"}`) as an MCP env or header value caused `config validate` to return `Invalid input`. This PR widens the accepted surface so both `env` and `headers` fields on core MCP servers store SecretInput values, with runtime resolution delivering the resolved string to the spawned MCP process.

- Real environment tested: Ubuntu 24.04 (HAL server), OpenClaw built from PR head `24e3416d75b` (runtime captured at parent `9598ab1` — `24e3416d75b` changes test-file module paths only, no runtime delta). 1Password CLI service account for secret backing.

- Exact steps or command run after this patch:
  1. Write `openclaw.json` with `mcp.servers.mission-control.env.MC_API_KEY = {"source":"env","id":"MC_API_KEY"}` and `mcp.servers.mission-control.headers.Authorization = {"source":"env","id":"MC_AUTH_TOKEN"}`
  2. `openclaw config validate`
  3. `openclaw secrets audit` (env vars absent — SecretRefs unresolved)
  4. `MC_API_KEY=[REDACTED] MC_AUTH_TOKEN=[REDACTED] ANTHROPIC_API_KEY=[REDACTED] openclaw secrets audit`

- Evidence after fix (copied terminal output, values redacted):

  ```
  $ openclaw --version
  OpenClaw 2026.6.2 (9598ab1)

  $ openclaw config validate
  Config valid: /tmp/proof/state/openclaw.json

  $ openclaw secrets audit
  Secrets audit: unresolved. plaintext=0, unresolved=3, shadowed=0, legacy=0.
  - [REF_UNRESOLVED] openclaw.json:mcp.servers.mission-control.env.MC_API_KEY
      Failed to resolve env:default:MC_API_KEY (variable missing)
  - [REF_UNRESOLVED] openclaw.json:mcp.servers.mission-control.headers.Authorization
      Failed to resolve env:default:MC_AUTH_TOKEN (variable missing)
  - [REF_UNRESOLVED] openclaw.json:models.providers.anthropic.apiKey
      Failed to resolve env:default:ANTHROPIC_API_KEY (variable missing)

  $ MC_API_KEY=[REDACTED] MC_AUTH_TOKEN=[REDACTED] ANTHROPIC_API_KEY=[REDACTED] openclaw secrets audit
  Secrets audit: clean. plaintext=0, unresolved=0, shadowed=0, legacy=0.
  ```

- Observed result after fix: Config validates cleanly with SecretRef objects in both `env.*` and `headers.*`. Secrets audit identifies `MC_API_KEY` and `Authorization` as unresolved SecretRefs and correctly ignores `MC_URL` (URL-shaped) and `X-Feature-Flag` (generic literal). With env vars set, audit is fully clean (`plaintext=0, unresolved=0`).

- What was not tested: Live end-to-end MCP spawn with SecretRef values resolved from exec provider (HAL `/tmp` is mounted `noexec`, blocking shell-script exec tests locally). MCP session runtime SecretRef injection is covered by unit tests in `pi-bundle-mcp-runtime.test.ts`.

- Proof limitations or environment constraints: `/tmp` is mounted `noexec` on the test machine, preventing exec-provider shell-script invocation locally. Same exec tests pass in CI (GitHub Actions standard tmpfs is executable). Version shown is `9598ab1` (parent of `24e3416d75b`) — both commits produce identical runtime behavior; `24e3416d75b` only renames test-file module paths.

- Before evidence: Without this PR, `openclaw config validate` on the same config returned:
  ```
  OpenClaw config is invalid: /tmp/proof/state/openclaw.json
    x mcp.servers.mission-control.env.MC_API_KEY: Invalid input
    x mcp.servers.mission-control.headers.Authorization: Invalid input
  ```

## Scope

This PR is limited to core MCP SecretRef support for `mcp.servers` env/header surfaces and the related docs/tests needed to support that behavior.

It does not include unrelated runtime, deployment, or integration changes.

## AI assistance

AI-assisted.

Testing performed locally with repo checks, targeted tests, and direct runtime validation.

target.title: feat(doctor): detect local rebuild vs pristine npm release
target.body:
## Problem

A stock `openclaw update` silently overwrites locally patched installs.
Regressions return quietly because operators have no signal that the
running package has diverged from the pristine npm release.

Concrete trigger: the 2026-04-20 dist-overlay audit of
`/usr/lib/node_modules/openclaw` on a production host showed
v2026.4.15 with `dist/build-info.json.builtAt = 2026-04-19` while the
npm registry reported that version was published on `2026-04-16`.
That ~3-day skew is a locally rebuilt install — a stock `update` had
wiped a sticky-fallback patch that had been applied by hand.

## Solution

Non-blocking `openclaw doctor` note that compares local build metadata
against the npm registry publish time and warns if the installed
package is a local rebuild, an unreleased version, or has corrupt
metadata.

* **Warn only.** Never fails doctor.
* **Silent on pristine or inconclusive** (no network, no cache, etc.).
* **Runs in the install-posture section** of `doctor`, right after
  `noteSourceInstallIssues`.

## Signals

* Reads `<packageRoot>/dist/build-info.json` → `{version, commit, builtAt}`
  and `<packageRoot>/package.json.version`.
* Fetches `https://registry.npmjs.org/openclaw`, inspects
  `.time[<version>]`.
* **rebuild** — `builtAt - publishedAt >= 60 min`.
* **unreleased** — version not present in registry `.time`.
* **corrupt** — local `build-info.json` / `package.json` missing or
  malformed.
* **inconclusive** — registry fetch failed and no usable cache
  (silent, to avoid false positives on air-gapped installs).
* **pristine** — otherwise.

Registry response cached at `~/.openclaw/rebuild-guard-cache.json`
with a 6h TTL. Honors `OPENCLAW_NPM_REGISTRY_FILE` for tests and an
`offline: true` option for environments that should never hit the
network.

## Test coverage

`src/commands/doctor-rebuild-guard.test.ts` (8/8 green):

* pristine within the skew window
* rebuild when `builtAt` is >= 60 min after npm publish
* unreleased (version not in registry)
* corrupt (missing / malformed `build-info.json`)
* inconclusive (fetch throws, no cache) → silent
* offline mode (no network call, decides from local only)
* cache hit (second call, within TTL, does not call `fetch`)
* `noteRebuildGuardHealth` emission matrix: emits for
  `rebuild` / `unreleased` / `corrupt`; silent for
  `pristine` / `inconclusive`

## Gates

* `pnpm lint` — pass
* `pnpm tsgo:all` (typecheck) — pass
* `pnpm build` — pass
* `pnpm exec vitest run src/commands/doctor-rebuild-guard.test.ts` — 8/8

## Sample doctor output (rebuilt host)

```
└ Install ────────────────────────────────────────
  Local OpenClaw install differs from the pristine npm release.
  - Install: /usr/lib/node_modules/openclaw
  - Version: 2026.4.15
  - Commit: 8abd1fc
  - builtAt: 2026-04-19T08:42:11Z
  - npm publish: 2026-04-16T19:03:47Z
  - Skew: 4197 minutes ahead of npm
  - Reason: rebuild-after-publish
  Confirm every local patch has a matching upstream PR before running `openclaw update`.
```

## Related

A companion **Layer-1 bash guard** already ships in operator
workspaces (installed as a systemd `ExecStartPre`) to surface the same
warning on daemon start. This PR makes the same check native to every
`openclaw` install so end users benefit from it too, not just
operators who maintain local workspaces.

target.title: add option for MCP apps support
target.body:
## Summary
 - Problem: OpenClaw calls MCP servers, but ignores MCP Apps UI metadata/resources
 - Why it matters: MCP Apps turns trusted MCP servers into richer tool providers with purpose-built UI. Without host support, OpenClaw misses that part of the MCP ecosystem even when the server is configured and callable.
- What changed: Added opt-in MCP Apps host support: parse UI metadata, hide app-only tools from the model, fetch MCP App resources into canvas documents, render them through the existing embed/canvas path with an app bridge, and proxy app-initiated MCP calls through the active session runtime.
- What changed: Added restart/reload hardening for restored app views, wider assistant canvas layout, docs, config schema support,tests, and a security-audit warning when `mcp.apps.enabled=true`.
-  What did NOT change (scope boundary): Normal MCP tool execution still works without MCP Apps. MCP Apps are default-off behind `mcp.apps.enabled`; this does not add a marketplace, new MCP server examples, or plugin-only architecture.

## Change Type (select all)

- [ ] Bug fix
- [x] Feature
- [ ] Refactor required for the fix
- [x] Docs
- [x] Security hardening
- [ ] Chore/infra

## Scope (select all touched areas)

- [x] Gateway / orchestration
- [x] Skills / tool execution
- [ ] Auth / tokens
- [ ] Memory / storage
- [x] Integrations
- [x] API / contracts
- [x] UI / DX
- [ ] CI/CD / infra

## Regression Test Plan (if applicable)
- MCP app-only tools are not materialized for the model.
- MCP Apps UI payloads are only fetched/rendered when `mcp.apps.enabled=true`.
- App bridge calls are rejected when MCP Apps are disabled.
- App bridge calls enforce app-callable tool visibility.
- Persisted/restored app views can recreate an MCP runtime after gateway restart.
- Existing `[embed]` canvas rendering remains the fallback path when MCP Apps metadata is absent or disabled.

- Target test or file:
  - New opt-in config: `mcp.apps.enabled`.
  - Default behavior is unchanged for normal MCP tools; MCP Apps UI rendering is disabled by default.
  - When enabled, trusted MCP servers can render interactive app views in chat using the existing canvas/embed path.
  - MCP Apps views may call app-visible MCP tools through the active session runtime.
  - App-only MCP tools are hidden from the model.
  - `openclaw security audit` now warns when MCP Apps are enabled.
  - Docs now describe MCP Apps setup, scope, and security considerations. 
- Scenario the test should lock in:
    - MCP Apps metadata is preserved from MCP tool catalogs through tool results and chat rendering.
    - App-only tools are hidden from the model.
    - Model-only tools are blocked from app bridge calls.
    - MCP Apps UI rendering and bridge proxy calls require `mcp.apps.enabled=true`.
    - Restored app views can recreate an MCP runtime after gateway restart.
    - Existing canvas/embed rendering remains the fallback when MCP Apps are disabled or metadata is absent.
- Why this is the smallest reliable guardrail:
    - The feature crosses runtime materialization, gateway proxying, config/bootstrap, and UI rendering. Focused unit and seam tests cover those boundaries without requiring a live browser, real MCP server fleet, or container e2e in normal CI.
- Existing test that already covers this (if any):
    - Existing canvas/embed tests covered generic iframe rendering, but not MCP Apps metadata, app-only visibility, app bridge calls, or restored MCP runtime behavior.

  ## User-visible / Behavior Changes
  - New opt-in config: `mcp.apps.enabled`.
  - Default behavior is unchanged for normal MCP tools; MCP Apps UI rendering is disabled by default.
  - When enabled, trusted MCP servers can render interactive app views in chat using the existing canvas/embed path.
  - MCP Apps views may call app-visible MCP tools through the active session runtime.
  - App-only MCP tools are hidden from the model.
  - `openclaw security audit` now warns when MCP Apps are enabled.
  - Docs now describe MCP Apps setup, scope, and security considerations.
  
## Diagram (if applicable)

  ```mermaid
  flowchart LR
    A[MCP server tool] --> B[Tool result with UI metadata]
    B --> C[OpenClaw MCP runtime]
    C --> D[Fetch MCP App HTML resource]
    D --> E[Canvas document / existing embed path]
    E --> F[Control UI mcp-app-view iframe]
    F --> G[AppBridge]
    G --> H[Gateway mcp.* proxy]
    H --> C
  ```
## Security Impact (required)
  - New permissions/capabilities? (`Yes/No`): Yes
  - Secrets/tokens handling changed? (`Yes/No`): No
  - New/changed network calls? (`Yes/No`): No for normal MCP transport; Yes in the sense that enabled MCP Apps can make app-initiated MCP calls back through the existing MCP session runtime.
  - Command/tool execution surface changed? (`Yes/No`): Yes
  - Data access scope changed? (`Yes/No`): Yes
  - If any `Yes`, explain risk + mitigation:
    - Risks:
       - MCP servers with MCP Apps support can provide HTML/JS UI resources that OpenClaw renders in chat.
       -  rendered app views can call app-visible MCP tools and read MCP resources through the active session runtime.
       - app views receive the tool input/result payload they are rendering.
    - Mitigations:
        - MCP Apps are default-off behind `mcp.apps.enabled=true`.
        - app views render in sandboxed iframes using the existing Control UI embed sandbox policy.
        - app-only tools are hidden from the model, and model-only tools are blocked from app bridge calls.
        - app bridge calls are scoped to the active MCP session/server and exposed through narrow `mcp.*` gateway methods.
        - `openclaw security audit` warns when MCP Apps are enabled.
- Trust model: enabling MCP Apps means trusting the configured MCP servers not only as tool providers, but also as UI code providers.

  ## Repro + Verification

  ### Environment
  - OS: macOS
  - Runtime/container: local OpenClaw container via Podman
  - Model/provider: Claude Sonnet 4.6
  - Integration/channel (if any): Control UI chat with configured MCP servers
  - Relevant config (redacted):

```json
  {
    "mcp": {
      "apps": {
        "enabled": true
      },
      "servers": {
        "system-monitor": {
          "command": "npx",
          "args": ["-y", "@modelcontextprotocol/server-system-monitor", "--stdio"]
        },
        "pdf": {
          "command": "npx",
          "args": ["-y", "@modelcontextprotocol/server-pdf", "--stdio"]
        },
        "qr": {
          "url": "http://127.0.0.1:3108/mcp",
          "transport": "streamable-http"
        }
      }
    }
  }
```

### Steps
  1. Build and run an OpenClaw image from this branch.
  2. Enable MCP Apps with openclaw config set mcp.apps.enabled true --strict-json.
  3. Configure the system-monitor, pdf, and qr MCP servers.
  4. Start the QR MCP sidecar if using the QR example.
  5. Start a fresh chat/session in the Control UI.
  6. Prompt the agent to show live system info using the system-monitor MCP Apps UI.
  7. Prompt the agent to generate a QR code using the QR MCP Apps UI.
  8. Prompt the agent to display https://arxiv.org/pdf/1706.03762 using the PDF MCP Apps UI.
  9. Restart the OpenClaw container and reload the Control UI.
  10. Confirm the persisted PDF app view reloads and reconnects.

### Expected

  - Normal MCP tools still work.
  - With mcp.apps.enabled=true, MCP Apps UI resources render as interactive embedded app views.
  - System monitor renders a live monitor UI.
  - QR renders an embedded QR UI.
  - PDF renders an interactive PDF viewer.
  - Restored app views can reconnect after gateway/container restart.
  - With mcp.apps.enabled=false, app bridge calls are rejected and MCP Apps render through the normal fallback path instead of the app bridge.

### Actual

  - Verified locally with system-monitor, QR, and PDF MCP Apps examples.
  - Verified upstream image comparison: upstream can call the MCP servers but does not render the MCP Apps UI resources.
  - Verified restart/reload behavior for the PDF app view.

Attach at least one:

- [ ] Failing test/log before + passing after
- [ ] Trace/log snippets
- [x] Screenshot/recording
- [ ] Perf numbers (if relevant)

## Human Verification (required)

https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/aaa93975-4406-493e-a060-4ac64763704b

## Review Conversations

- [ ] I replied to or resolved every bot review conversation I addressed in this PR.
- [ ] I left unresolved only the conversations that still need reviewer or maintainer judgment.

If a bot review conversation is addressed by this PR, resolve that conversation yourself. Do not leave bot review conversation cleanup for maintainers.

## Compatibility / Migration

- Backward compatible? (`Yes/No`) Yes
- Config/env changes? (`Yes/No`) Yes
- Migration needed? (`Yes/No`) No
- If yes, exact upgrade steps:
    - No migration is required for existing installs. MCP Apps support is default-off.
    - To enable it, set:
      ```bash
      openclaw config set mcp.apps.enabled true --strict-json
      ```
    - Configure MCP servers that expose MCP Apps / `mcp-ui` resources.
    - Restart OpenClaw so the updated config is loaded.

## Risks and Mitigations

  - Risk: Enabling MCP Apps allows configured MCP servers to provide HTML/JS UI that renders inside OpenClaw.
    - Mitigation: Feature is opt-in via `mcp.apps.enabled`; app UI renders in a sandboxed iframe; docs and security audit call out the trust boundary.
  - Risk: App views can call app-visible MCP tools/resources through the bridge, increasing the practical surface area of a trusted MCP server.
    - Mitigation: Bridge is scoped to MCP app methods only; model-only tools are blocked from app bridge calls; app-only tools are hidden from the model.
  - Risk: Restored chat views may need to reconnect to MCP runtimes after a container restart.
    - Mitigation: Gateway proxy lazily recreates the session MCP runtime from config when an app bridge call arrives and no runtime is currently active.
  - Risk: Servers without MCP Apps support, or installs with the feature disabled, should not regress.
    - Mitigation: Existing MCP tool execution path remains unchanged; UI falls back to the existing embed/canvas rendering behavior when MCP Apps are disabled or metadata is absent.

target.title: fix: restrict trajectory export to owners [AI]
target.body:
## What Problem This Solves

Fixes an issue where authorized channel users who were not owners could start a trajectory export from chat and receive sensitive session artifacts.

## Why This Change Was Made

Trajectory export now uses the same owner-only command gate as other sensitive chat commands before building the export reply. The change keeps the existing authorized-sender check and adds the owner check at the command handler boundary, so the export builder is not reached for non-owner senders.

## User Impact

Owners can still export trajectory bundles from the active session. Authorized non-owner users can no longer initiate that export path from chat.

## Evidence

- `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/auto-reply/reply/commands-info.test.ts` passed: 1 file, 17 tests.

AI-assisted: yes.

target.title: feat(googlechat): add sessionThread option to bind OpenClaw sessions to Google Chat threads
target.body: AI-assisted: yes (Claude Opus 4.7). ## Summary - Problem: In Google Chat, all messages in a space shared a single OpenClaw session. Starting a new thread to change topic still inherited memory from prior threads, and replies did not reliably land in the user's inbound thread. - Why it matters: Chat threads are the natural topic boundary in Google Chat. Leaking context across threads produces confusing replies ("why does the bot remember something from a different thread?"), forces multiple concurrent threads to queue sequentially on one session, and breaks the mental model users already have from Slack. Requested in #13373. - What changed: Added an opt-in `sessionThread` channel option. When `true`, the OpenClaw session peer id is derived from the inbound thread name, so each Google Chat thread is its own conversation. Replies are now threaded via `thread.name` + `messageReplyOption=REPLY_MESSAGE_FALLBACK_TO_NEW_THREAD`, so bot responses land in the user's thread. - What did NOT change (scope boundary): Default behavior is unchanged (`sessionThread: false` keeps space-scoped sessions). No changes to auth, allowlists, webhook routing, DM flow, reactions, or attachments. No slash commands / `/focus`-style bindings added (see "future work" below). ## Change Type (select all) - [x] Bug fix - [x] Feature - [ ] Refactor required for the fix - [x] Docs - [ ] Security hardening - [ ] Chore/infra ## Scope (select all touched areas) - [ ] Gateway / orchestration - [ ] Skills / tool execution - [ ] Auth / tokens - [ ] Memory / storage - [x] Integrations - [ ] API / contracts - [ ] UI / DX - [ ] CI/CD / infra ## Linked Issue/PR - Closes #13373 (per-thread session mode feature request) - Closes #41788 (Google Chat replies creating new threads instead of staying in the existing one) - Related #44347 — resolves the first of the two requests in that issue - [x] This PR fixes a bug or regression ### Note on naming vs the issue's proposal The issue proposes `threadSessionMode: "per-thread" | "per-space"`. This PR ships the same behavior under a boolean flag `sessionThread: boolean` (default `false` = per-space, `true` = per-thread). Rationale: - A boolean matches the two-state nature of the option and is simpler to document. - The name aligns with the existing session-scoping vocabulary in the codebase. - Happy to rename to `threadSessionMode` with a string enum if maintainers prefer the issue's exact wording, it's a straightforward rename. ## Root Cause (if applicable) - Root cause: The Google Chat channel used the space id as the session peer id and did not pass the inbound thread name back on outbound messages. That coupling meant (a) all threads in a space mapped to one session, and (b) replies could fall back to a new thread instead of the user's. - Missing detection / guardrail: No peer-id unit coverage for Google Chat; Slack already has `prepare.thread-session-key.test.ts` as prior art for this exact pattern, but nothing equivalent existed for Google Chat. - Contributing context (if known): Google Chat threads were less prominent historically in OpenClaw flows; the space-scoped default was acceptable until users started relying on threads to separate topics. ## Regression Test Plan (if applicable) - Coverage level that should have caught this: - [x] Unit test - [ ] Seam / integration test - [ ] End-to-end test - [ ] Existing coverage already sufficient - Target test or file: `extensions/googlechat/src/monitor.session-thread.test.ts` - Scenario the test should lock in: `resolveGoogleChatPeerId` returns the thread name when `sessionThread` is enabled and an inbound thread is present; falls back to `spaceId` otherwise; and produces distinct peer ids for distinct threads so sessions do not cross. - Why this is the smallest reliable guardrail: The peer id is the single point where "which session does this message belong to?" is decided for Google Chat. Locking its behavior at the helper boundary catches the regression without pulling in the full monitor stack. - Existing test that already covers this (if any): None for Google Chat. Slack's `prepare.thread-session-key.test.ts` covers the analogous primitive. ## User-visible / Behavior Changes - New opt-in config: `channels.googlechat.sessionThread` (default `false`). - `false` (default): space-scoped sessions, prior behavior unchanged. - `true`: one OpenClaw session per Google Chat thread; a new thread starts a fresh conversation. - Outbound messages now consistently target the inbound thread via `thread.name` + `messageReplyOption=REPLY_MESSAGE_FALLBACK_TO_NEW_THREAD`, so replies land in the user's thread more reliably. This applies regardless of `sessionThread`. ## Diagram (if applicable) ```text sessionThread: false (default, unchanged) space S, thread A -> session(peer=S) space S, thread B -> session(peer=S) # shared memory sessionThread: true space S, thread A -> session(peer=A) space S, thread B -> session(peer=B) # isolated memory space S, no thread -> session(peer=S) # falls back to space-scoped ``` ## Security Impact (required) - New permissions/capabilities? No - Secrets/tokens handling changed? No - New/changed network calls? No (same Chat API endpoints; only request payload now sets `thread.name` and `messageReplyOption`) - Command/tool execution surface changed? No - Data access scope changed? No (in fact, `sessionThread: true` narrows memory scope per thread) ## Repro + Verification ### Environment - OS: macOS - Runtime/container: Node 22 / local gateway (`pnpm openclaw gateway restart`) - Model/provider: any (provider-agnostic) - Integration/channel (if any): Google Chat - Relevant config (redacted): ```jsonc { "channels": { "googlechat": { "enabled": true, "serviceAccountFile": "/path/to/service-account.json", "sessionThread": true } } } ``` ### Steps 1. In a Google Chat space, mention the bot in thread A and ask an identifying question (e.g. "remember the color red"). 2. Start a new thread B in the same space and ask "what color did I mention?". 3. Reply again inside thread A and ask the same question. ### Expected - `sessionThread: true`: in thread B, the bot has no memory of thread A. In thread A, the bot still remembers "red". Replies land in the thread the user messaged in. - `sessionThread: false` (default): behavior is unchanged from prior releases. ### Actual - Matches expected for both modes. ## Evidence - [x] Failing test/log before + passing after ``` pnpm test extensions/googlechat/src/monitor.session-thread.test.ts ✓ resolveGoogleChatPeerId returns spaceId when sessionThread is disabled ✓ resolveGoogleChatPeerId returns spaceId when sessionThread is undefined (default) ✓ resolveGoogleChatPeerId returns thread name when sessionThread is enabled and inbound has a thread ✓ resolveGoogleChatPeerId falls back to spaceId when sessionThread is enabled but inbound has no thread ✓ resolveGoogleChatPeerId produces distinct peer ids for distinct threads so sessions don't cross Test Files 1 passed (1) Tests 5 passed (5) ``` - [ ] Trace/log snippets - [x] Screenshot/recording - When disabled(previous behavior): - When enabled: - [ ] Perf numbers (if relevant) ## Human Verification (required) - Verified scenarios: - Manually verified in a real Google Chat space against a local gateway: - Bot replies land inside the user's inbound thread (both existing threads and thread-starter messages). - With `sessionThread: true`: new thread = fresh session; prior thread keeps its own context; no cross-thread memory leakage. - With `sessionThread: false`: unchanged, space-scoped memory. - Edge cases checked: - Inbound message with no thread (thread-starter): falls back to space-scoped session as designed. - Two concurrent threads in the same space maintain separate sessions. - What you did **not** verify: - Bot-initiated outbound into a space with no prior inbound thread context (no webhook-origin thread). This PR intentionally does not introduce a `threadKey` fallback for that case; the follow-up work below can add richer binding if demand emerges. - DM flow (no behavior change expected; DM scope is already per-channel-peer). ## Review Conversations - [x] I replied to or resolved every bot review conversation I addressed in this PR. - [x] I left unresolved only the conversations that still need reviewer or maintainer judgment. ## Compatibility / Migration - Backward compatible? Yes - Config/env changes? Yes, additive only: new optional `channels.googlechat.sessionThread` boolean (default `false`). - Migration needed? No. Existing configs continue to behave as before. - If yes, exact upgrade steps: N/A. To opt in, set `channels.googlechat.sessionThread: true`. ## Risks and Mitigations - Risk: Users relying on cross-thread memory in a space would lose that if they set `sessionThread: true` without realizing the implication. - Mitigation: Default is `false`; docs call out the per-thread isolation explicitly; the name `sessionThread` signals the intent. - Risk: `resolveGoogleChatPeerId` is a new exported helper surface from the plugin's `monitor.ts`. - Mitigation: Kept it narrow (one pure function) and it is only consumed inside the extension itself; no public SDK subpath added. ## Future Work (non-blocking) This PR adds the minimal primitive, matching Slack's auto thread-scoped session behavior, just gated behind an opt-in flag for Google Chat. A follow-up could align with the Telegram/Discord `session.threadBindings` model (`/focus`, `/unfocus`, `/session idle`, `/session max-age`) on top of this primitive. That work requires registering slash commands in the Chat API app config and wiring `SLASH_COMMAND` events, which is out of scope here.

target.title: fix(acp): treat missing cwd as stale bound session
target.body:
## Summary

- Problem: a bound ACP session can stay attached after its persisted runtime working directory disappears, so retries keep failing against a dead session and the conversation stays wedged.
- Why it matters: affected conversations remain wedged until the stale binding state is manually cleared.
- What changed: during ACP dispatch, treat a generic `ACP_SESSION_INIT_FAILED` as stale when the bound session's persisted `runtimeOptions.cwd` can no longer be resolved on disk (`ENOENT`/`ENOTDIR`), then unbind the dead conversation. The signal is folded into the existing `isStaleSessionInitError` classifier and the existing `maybeUnbindStaleBoundConversations` dispatch helper — no new module, no parallel helper.
- Scope: dispatch-only. Two files: `src/auto-reply/reply/dispatch-acp.ts` and its test.

## Change Type

- [x] Bug fix
- [ ] Refactor required for the fix
- [ ] Feature
- [ ] Docs
- [ ] Security hardening
- [ ] Chore/infra

## Scope

- ACP stale-session classifier (`isStaleSessionInitError`) extended to accept the bound session's persisted cwd.
- Auto-reply ACP dispatch stale-binding cleanup (`maybeUnbindStaleBoundConversations`) passes the persisted cwd at the `runTurn` catch.
- Regression tests: generic `ACP_SESSION_INIT_FAILED` + missing persisted cwd unbinds; generic init failure + existing cwd does not.

## Linked Issue/PR

- Related #60720
- [x] This PR fixes a bug or regression

## Root Cause

The dispatch path already removes stale ACP bindings for selected ACP session init failures, but it classified staleness from the error message only. The live missing-cwd case surfaces as a generic spawn `ENOENT` wrapped as `ACP_SESSION_INIT_FAILED`, whose message does not match the stale-message set. The reliable signal is the bound session's persisted `runtimeOptions.cwd`: when it no longer resolves to a directory, the binding is unrecoverable. Without checking it, the stale bound conversation survives the failed dispatch and keeps routing future turns into the dead ACP session.

## Implementation Notes

- Rebased onto current `main` after the `@openclaw/acp-core` runtime extraction. The fix now extends `main`'s existing in-file `maybeUnbindStaleBoundConversations`/`isStaleSessionInitError` instead of introducing a separate `src/acp/runtime/stale-session.ts` helper (which no longer fits the post-extraction layout). The earlier `extensions/memory-core` test shim is dropped — current `main` carries that mock contract natively.
- Staleness now requires **filesystem-confirmed** absence of the persisted cwd. The unbind cannot be triggered by an error-message string alone, which removes an over-removal vector (e.g. a transient tool error whose text mentions a missing working directory). `ENOENT` and `ENOTDIR` count as missing; a present-but-unreadable cwd (`EACCES`) is treated as present so a transient permission/mount issue never severs a live conversation.

## Maintainer Decision (session-state semantics)

This removes persisted bound-conversation state when a generic `ACP_SESSION_INIT_FAILED` is paired with a filesystem-confirmed missing cwd — the recovery is correct for a permanently gone cwd, but it is session-state behavior worth explicit maintainer acceptance (per ClawSweeper's `merge-risk: session-state`). Two residual, deliberately out-of-scope behaviors to weigh:

- A transient filesystem outage (mount briefly returning `ENOENT`) on a still-valid cwd would unbind a live session. Mitigated by treating only `ENOENT`/`ENOTDIR` (not `EACCES`) as missing, but not eliminated.
- `unbind({ targetSessionKey })` removes all bindings for that session (pre-existing helper scope). For a shared-cwd session that is arguably correct, since the cwd is shared by all its bindings.

## Real behavior proof

- Behavior or issue addressed: A bound ACP session whose persisted runtime `cwd` no longer exists should be treated as stale during dispatch cleanup, unbinding the affected conversation instead of keeping retries stuck on the same dead session.
- Real environment tested: Disposable local OpenClaw gateway from this PR checkout on NixOS Linux, Telegram DM via staging bot `@kesslerClawBot`, loopback gateway port `18794`, cron/heartbeats disabled. Gateway was stopped after the proof run.
- Exact steps or command run after this patch: Started the disposable staging gateway from PR head `1ad568ba1e0db9051904eacee025ee36ec2f06ee`, sent a Telegram DM to `@kesslerClawBot` that routed through a bound ACP conversation whose persisted `runtimeOptions.cwd` pointed at `/home/art/.local/state/openclaw-staging/disposable/missing-cwd-pr68843`, let ACP dispatch fail with generic `ACP_SESSION_INIT_FAILED`, then inspected the post-run binding state.
- Evidence after fix (screenshot, recording, terminal capture, console output, redacted runtime log, linked artifact, or copied live output): Redacted public proof artifact: https://gist.github.com/kesslerio/dbefdac0c5d5a5849997b6bf702047c6. Follow-up proof comment: https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/pull/68843#issuecomment-4558022819.

  ```txt
  2026-05-27T12:18:57.491-07:00 telegram: routed via bound conversation 8352721935 -> agent:niemand-code:acp:binding:telegram:default:ecf1b7272077d0d1
  2026-05-27T12:18:57.520-07:00 telegram inbound: chatId=8352721935 ... preview="[...] PR68843 patched missing cwd cleanup proof"
  2026-05-27T12:19:06.365-07:00 [plugins] embedded acpx runtime backend ready
  2026-05-27T12:19:06.400-07:00 dispatch-acp: removed 1 stale bound conversation(s) for agent:niemand-code:acp:binding:telegram:default:ecf1b7272077d0d1 after ACP_SESSION_INIT_FAILED: Failed to spawn agent command: /run/current-system/sw/bin/codex-acp-openclaw
  2026-05-27T12:19:06.404-07:00 acp-dispatch: session=agent:niemand-code:acp:binding:telegram:default:ecf1b7272077d0d1 outcome=error code=ACP_SESSION_INIT_FAILED latencyMs=327 queueDepth=0 activeRuntimes=0
  ```

  Post-run binding state:

  ```json
  {
    "bindingCount": 0,
    "sessionKey": "agent:niemand-code:acp:binding:telegram:default:ecf1b7272077d0d1",
    "acpState": "error",
    "runtimeOptions": {
      "cwd": "/home/art/.local/state/openclaw-staging/disposable/missing-cwd-pr68843"
    }
  }
  ```

- Observed result after fix: the staging Telegram DM routed through the stale bound ACP conversation, ACPX reached the runtime backend, dispatch failed with generic `ACP_SESSION_INIT_FAILED`, and the stale bound conversation was removed because the persisted ACP cwd was missing. Post-run binding state showed `bindingCount: 0`.
- What was not tested: Production AlphaClaw was not used. This proof uses the disposable staging gateway and staging Telegram bot to avoid disrupting the live Telegram poller. A broader channel-neutral ACP proof was not run because the Telegram staging proof exercises the dispatch cleanup path directly.
- Proof limitations or environment constraints: the raw local `/home/art/...` gateway log is retained only as an operator audit pointer because it contains unnecessary routing identifiers and machine-specific details. Public proof is the redacted gist and copied transcript above.
- Before evidence (optional but encouraged): current `main` only treated selected ACP init-failure messages as stale and did not inspect persisted ACP session `cwd` metadata for a generic `ACP_SESSION_INIT_FAILED`; ClawSweeper confirmed the source-level pre-fix path in its review comment.

## Tests and validation

Commands run on rebased head `79c9e2392d` (worktree off current `origin/main`):

```sh
pnpm exec oxfmt --check --threads=1 src/auto-reply/reply/dispatch-acp.ts src/auto-reply/reply/dispatch-acp.test.ts
node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/auto-reply/reply/dispatch-acp.test.ts -t "stale|cwd"
node scripts/run-tsgo.mjs -p test/tsconfig/tsconfig.core.test.json --incremental --tsBuildInfoFile .artifacts/tsgo-cache/core-test.tsbuildinfo
```

Results: formatting passed; focused stale/cwd dispatch suite passed (5 passed, 50 skipped — including the new missing-cwd-unbinds and existing-cwd-does-not-unbind cases); core test typecheck passed (exit 0).

## Security Impact

No new permissions, secrets handling, network calls, command execution surface, or data access scope. The behavioral expansion is limited to unbinding stale ACP conversations after an ACP init failure when the persisted ACP cwd is filesystem-confirmed missing.

## Compatibility / Migration

No config or migration required. The change affects only stale ACP init failure recovery.

## AI Assistance

AI-assisted rebase onto current `main`, fold-in to the existing dispatch helper, structural/correctness/adversarial review hardening, validation, and PR-body update. The live staging proof above remains valid: it unbound via the generic `Failed to spawn agent command` failure plus the missing-cwd probe, which is exactly the path this rebased head preserves.
